retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: For unresectable disease, platinum-based chemotherapy agents, such as cisplatin, remain the primary treatment option. However, malignant pleural mesothelioma exhibits significant resistance to chemotherapy, resulting in an uncertain survival benefit. For instance, the combination of gemcitabine and cisplatin demonstrates a response rate of 12% to 48%, yet the median overall survival remains only 9 to 13 months. Ongoing research is exploring biologic and antiangiogenic therapies as potential advancements in treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Nivolumab -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Non–small cell lung cancer Metastatic, with progression during or after platinum-based chemotherapy (and after progression under targeted therapies for EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations in case these aberrations are present). [2] As first-line therapy combined with ipilimumab in metastatic or recurrent non–small cell lung cancer with a PD-L1 expression equal or higher to 1% with no EGFR or ALK genomic tumor aberrations. [3] As first-line therapy in combination with ipilimumab, given with 2 cycles of platinum-doublet chemotherapy. Renal cell carcinoma After prior anti­angiogenic therapy. Previously untreated, of intermediate or poor risk, in combination with ipilimumab. [4] Squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck Recurrent or metastatic with progressive disease on or following a platinum-based therapy. [5] Pleural Mesothelioma In combination with ipilimumab for unresectable pleural mesothelioma. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The FDA recently changed the pembrolizumab NSCLC monotherapy approval label to include PD-L1 expressers greater than 1% (from greater than 50%). However, without adequate head-to-head studies, pembrolizumab may not provide optimal patient outcomes compared to other approved treatment options in patients with PD-L1 expression between 1 and 49%. [26] Until better biomarkers and additional clinical trial data become available, it is unclear if the original label or the updated label is a better treatment regimen for these patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: It is recommended that mesothelioma be treated by an interprofessional team at a high-volume center. Many patients, often frail and diagnosed late, may benefit from palliative or hospice care as the prognosis is poor, with most surviving only a few months after diagnosis is made. Even though surgery is performed, it is associated with severe life-threatening complications. The majority of patients with this malignancy succumb within 12 months. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: According to the National Comprehensive Cancer Network Guidelines, treatment decisions are based on tumor staging, with stage III and IV malignant pleural mesothelioma considered unresectable. Less than a third of patients are candidates for definitive resection.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant mesothelioma is a complex disease that requires an interprofessional team, includeing an oncologist, thoracic surgeon, pulmonary specialist, radiation oncologist, and pain specialist. The cancer is difficult to diagnose, and current treatments have a limited impact on life expectancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Pembrolizumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In NSCLC, for example, Brody et al. conducted a large meta-analysis of thirty-five different clinical trials and showed many studies that linked higher PD-L1 expression with worse survival, but also several other studies that showed no such effect. [33] Additionally, PD-L1 as a predictive biomarker for checkpoint inhibitor treatment effect showed better success in higher expressers in some studies, but also no association in several other studies. [33] Consequently, although response and survival can correlate with PD-L1 expression, alternative methods are needed to stratify which specific patients are most likely to benefit. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Chemotherapy: The standard regimen combines pemetrexed with either cisplatin or carboplatin, often with folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation to reduce toxicity. In eligible patients, the addition of bevacizumab, a VEGF inhibitor, may enhance treatment efficacy. Immunotherapy: Combination immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor) and ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor), are FDA-approved for unresectable mesothelioma, demonstrating improved overall survival in clinical trials. Single-agent PD-1 inhibitors like pembrolizumab may be considered for refractory cases. 3. Radiation therapy Adjuvant radiation: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) is often used postoperatively to reduce the risk of local recurrence, particularly following an extrapleural pneumonectomy (EPP). Palliative radiation: This is primarily used for symptom management, including alleviating chest wall pain or controlling disease-related dyspnea. [11] 4. Emerging and investigational therapies

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite advancements, the prognosis for most patients with malignant pleural mesothelioma remains poor, with a median survival rate of 4 to 6 months without treatment. With appropriate treatment, some patients may achieve survival times of 15 to 18 months, and in some rare cases, 5-year survival has been reported. Tumor recurrence, particularly in surgically managed patients, remains a significant challenge to long-term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Ipilimumab -- Administration -- Malignant Pleural Mesothelioma
retrieved_docs.content: 1 mg/kg/dose IV every 6 weeks for up to 2 years; used with nivolumab. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Mesothelioma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment plan for mesothelioma should involve a multidisciplinary team, including surgical oncologists, medical oncologists, and radiation specialists. Enrollment in clinical trials is encouraged for eligible patients to access innovative therapies. It is advised that mesothelioma be managed by a multidisciplinary team at a high-volume center for optimal outcomes. [8] [14] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The long-term outcomes of TTF are still not available, but the initial results have shown promise. TTF has been FDA approved for use in recurrent glioblastoma, and ongoing trials may establish its role in not only primary glioblastoma but other malignancies like pancreatic cancer, ovarian cancer, non-small cell lung carcinoma, and mesothelioma. It may also play a role in modulating the effect of currently accepted treatment modalities like chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and immunotherapy. The fine-tuning of physical parameters involved in TTF with further research on TTF dosimetry is likely to enhance the impact of this novel modality in cancer therapeutics.

retrieved_docs.content: providing a targeted therapy for Hodgkin lymphoma and systemic anaplastic large‑cell lymphoma. Doxorubicin hydrochloride is an anthracycline chemotherapy agent that intercalates DNA and inhibits topoisomerase II, while its liposomal formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome) offers altered pharmacokinetics and reduced cardiotoxicity for various solid tumors and hematologic malignancies. Pembrolizumab is a programmed death‑1 (PD‑1) immune checkpoint inhibitor that enhances antitumor immunity in a wide range of cancers, including melanoma, non‑small cell lung cancer, and urothelial carcinoma. Sargramostim is a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor (GM‑CSF) used to accelerate recovery of white blood cells after chemotherapy or bone‑marrow transplantation. Vinblastine sulfate and vinorelbin tartarát are vinca alkaloid chemotherapeutics that disrupt microtubule formation, with vinblastine commonly used for Hodgkin lymphoma and testicular cancer, and vinorelbine

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host immunity. Collectively, these agents—ranging from traditional chemotherapeutics and central‑nervous‑system depressants to modern immunotherapies—illustrate the comprehensive pharmacologic approach to Hodgkin disease, emphasizing individualized regimen selection to optimize patient outcomes and underscoring the importance of systematic drug classification for clinical research and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 531
retrieved_docs.content: Conventional alkylating and antimetabolite chemotherapies—chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, mechlorethamine, procarbazine (including its hydrochloride form), dakarbazin and lomustine—exert cytotoxic effects on rapidly dividing Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Platinum‑based therapy with cisplatin and anthracycline antibiotics doxorubicin hydrochloride (both standard and liposomal formulations) add DNA‑damaging mechanisms. Additional cytotoxic agents used in Hodgkin protocols include bleomycin sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate and streptozocin, each targeting distinct aspects of tumor proliferation and survival. Targeted and immunomodulatory drugs expand therapeutic options: brentuximab vedotin delivers an anti‑CD30 antibody‑drug conjugate directly to Hodgkin lymphoma cells; pembrolizumab, a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor, enhances antitumor immune responses; and sargramostim, a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor, can augment host

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, cisplatina, lomustin, mechlorethamin, vincristine sulfate, bleomycin sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, pembrolizumab, streptozocin, and vinblastine sulfate are therapeutic agents that may be employed in the treatment of مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a diverse array of pharmacologic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, lomustin, mechlorethamin, streptozocin), a platinum compound (cisplatina), vinca alkaloids (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate), a bleomycin family agent (bleomycin sulfate), an anthracycline formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab). Their combined or sequential use reflects the multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to eradicate malignant Reed‑Sternberg cells, achieve durable remission, and minimize disease‑related morbidity. Selection of a specific regimen is guided by disease stage, prior therapies, and individual patient tolerance, underscoring the importance of personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 723
retrieved_docs.content: and chlormethin hydrochlorid further contribute to the chemotherapeutic arsenal. Targeted and immunomodulatory therapies are highlighted by brentuximab vedotin (a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate), pembrolizumab (a PD‑1 checkpoint inhibitor) and sargramostim (a recombinant granulocyte‑macrophage colony‑stimulating factor), each directing immune mechanisms against Hodgkin lymphoma cells. Collectively, these relationships illustrate a multimodal treatment paradigm that integrates alkylators, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, antimitotics, antibiotics, monoclonal antibodies and immune‑stimulating agents—used alone or in combination—to manage Hodgkin disease, underscoring the importance of diverse chemotherapy regimens and emerging immunotherapies in contemporary patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: coal‑tar 50 mg/mL medicated shampoo contains ethanol, underscoring alcohol’s role as a solvent in dermatologic formulations. The procedural hierarchy “Procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals” includes the specific intervention “administration of inhaled nitric oxide.” Cytotoxic agents such as doxorubicin, procarbazine, ifosfamide, streptozocin, vinblastine and vinorelbine exert antitumor effects by inducing DNA damage or disrupting microtubule dynamics, while pembrolizumab provides PD‑1 checkpoint inhibition and sargramostim offers colony‑stimulating‑factor‑mediated immunomodulation. Collectively, these diverse drug classes can be used alone or in combination to personalize therapeutic strategies for patients with Hodgkin disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is identified as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor, placing it within the product class of selective adhesion molecule inhibitors. In addition, crizanlizumab is classified as both an immunomodulatory agent (imunomodulátory) and an immunosuppressive agent (imunosupresiva), reflecting its dual capacity to modify and dampen immune system activity. These properties align crizanlizumab with the broader disposition of immunomodulators, indicating that its therapeutic action involves regulation of immune responses through targeted inhibition of adhesion molecules. This integrated classification underscores crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune pathways and its potential utility in conditions where controlling immune cell adhesion and activity is clinically beneficial.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1685
retrieved_docs.content: Cisplatina, dakarbazin, lomustin, mechlorethamin, prokarbazin, brentuximab vedotin, doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu, pembrolizumab, sargramostim, and vinblastine sulfate are all identified as agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين. These drugs represent a diverse set of therapeutic classes, including platinum‑based chemotherapy (cisplatina), alkylating agents (dakarbazin, lomustin, mechlorethamin, prokarbazin, hydrochlorid prokarbazinu), a liposomal anthracycline formulation (doxorubicin hydrochloride liposome), an antibody‑drug conjugate (brentuximab vedotin), an immune‑checkpoint inhibitor (pembrolizumab), a cytokine therapy (sargramostim), and a vinca alkaloid (vinblastine sulfate). Their potential use reflects a multimodal approach to managing مرض هودجكين, where conventional cytotoxic chemotherapy can be combined with targeted and immunomodulatory therapies to improve response rates and reduce relapse. Selection of a specific regimen depends on disease stage, prior treatment exposure, and individual patient factors, underscoring the importance of personalized care in the treatment of مرض هودجكين.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, lomustine (lomustin), mechlorethamine, procarbazine, bleomycin sulfate, chlormethine hydrochloride, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, hydrochloride procarbazine, pembrolizumab, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين). These drugs encompass a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, procarbazine, chlormethine hydrochloride), antitumor antibiotics (bleomycin sulfate), nitrogen mustard derivatives (mechlorethamine), platinum‑free cytotoxic agents (lomustine), vinca alkaloids (vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), and immune checkpoint inhibition (pembrolizumab). Their inclusion in treatment protocols reflects the multimodal approach required for effective management of Hodgkin disease, combining cytotoxic chemotherapy with targeted immunotherapy to improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacologic substances comprise both broad categories (e.g., barbiturates, ethanol) and numerous specific agents that are recorded as values for a genomic data commons (GDC) property, reflecting their clinical relevance. Individual drugs include ethinylestradiol; the alkylating agents bleomycin sulfate, chlorambucil, chlormethin hydrochloride, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, dacarbazine (dakarbazin), ifosfamide, lomustine, mechlorethamine, procarbazine (hydrochloride procarbazine), and streptozocin; the platinum‑based compound cisplatin; the anthracyclines doxorubicin hydrochloride and its liposomal formulation; the vinca‑alkaloid chemotherapeutics vinblastine, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine, and vinorelbine tartrate; the targeted antibody‑drug conjugate brentuximab vedotin; the immune‑checkpoint inhibitor pembrolizumab; and the recombinant colony‑stimulating factor sargramostim. These agents are collectively employed in the multimodal management of Hodgkin disease (مرض هودجكين), where traditional alkylators, platinum compounds, anthracyclines, vinca‑alkaloids, and newer therapies such as brentuximab vedotin and pembrolizumab are combined to achieve therapeutic effect, while sargramostim may support hematopoietic recovery. Additionally, a coal‑tar 50 mg/mL medicated shampoo contains ethanol, illustrating the use of ethanol in topical formulations rather than systemic oncology treatment. The inclusion of these diverse pharmacologic substances underscores their importance for data integration, informed patient‑care decisions, and the design of comprehensive chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and supportive‑care regimens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1948
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorambucil, procarbazine, vincristine sulfate, brentuximab vedotin, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, doxorubicin hydrochloride, ifosfamide, pembrolizumab, streptozocin, vinblastine sulfate, and vinorelbine tartrate are all agents that may be used to treat مرض هودجكين (Hodgkin disease). These drugs represent a range of therapeutic classes, including alkylating agents (chlorambucil, cyclophosphamide anhydrous, ifosfamide, procarbazine), anthracycline chemotherapy (doxorubicin hydrochloride), vinca‑alkaloid compounds (vincristine sulfate, vinblastine sulfate, vinorelbine tartrate), a nitrosourea derivative (streptozocin), a monoclonal antibody‑drug conjugate targeting CD30 (brentuximab vedotin), and an immune checkpoint inhibitor that blocks PD‑1 (pembrolizumab). The inclusion of both traditional cytotoxic chemotherapies and newer targeted or immunotherapeutic agents reflects the evolving multimodal approach to managing Hodgkin disease, aiming to improve response rates while balancing toxicity. Effective patient care involves selecting appropriate agents based on disease stage, prior treatment exposure, and individual patient factors, often combining several of these medications within established chemotherapy regimens or as part of salvage and maintenance strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1602
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is classified within the therapeutic class of Immunological Agents [TC] and is designated as an Immunomodulator (disposition). It is a specific type of Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of action that targets adhesion molecules to modulate immune cell interactions. Additionally, crizanlizumab is recognized as an imunosupresiva, indicating its broader role as an immunosuppressive agent. Together, these classifications emphasize crizanlizumab’s function as an immunological drug that modulates immune responses through selective inhibition of adhesion molecules, positioning it within both the immunomodulator and immunosuppressive categories of therapy.
